outcomes: Identify need for measurement and distinguish between fundamental and derived units within the SI system.
Apply rules for significant figures and determine uncertainty in experimental results.
Perform dimensional analysis to verify equations and derive relationships among physical quantities.
Describe motion in a straight line using elementary differentiation and integration techniques.
Interpret position-time and velocity-time graphs to analyze uniform and non-uniform motion.
Derive and apply relations for uniformly accelerated motion graphically and through calculus.

learning_outcomes.topic: Vector Algebra and Plane Motion Dynamics
outcomes: Perform addition, subtraction, multiplication by scalars, and resolve vectors into rectangular components in a plane.
Calculate scalar and vector products to solve physical problems involving directionality.
Analyze cases of uniform velocity and acceleration including projectile motion and uniform circular motion.
Explain intuitive concepts of force, inertia, and apply Newton's three laws to various scenarios.
Apply the law of conservation of linear momentum and analyze collisions using impulse-momentum theorem.
Analyze equilibrium conditions for concurrent forces and dynamics involving static/kinetic friction.
Calculate work done by constant and variable forces and apply the work-energy theorem.
Distinguish between conservative and non-conservative forces and analyze potential energy of springs.
Analyze elastic and inelastic collisions in one and two dimensions using conservation principles.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Motion of System of Particles and Rigid Body
learning_outcomes.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
outcomes: Determine the center of mass for particle systems and rigid bodies like uniform rods.
Apply laws of conservation of linear and angular momentum to solve dynamics problems.
Calculate torque, moment of inertia, and apply equations of rotational motion comparing them with linear counterparts.
Apply Kepler's laws and Newton's law of universal gravitation to planetary motion problems.
Analyze the variation of acceleration due to gravity with altitude and depth.
Calculate gravitational potential energy, escape speed, and orbital velocity for satellites.
Interpret stress-strain curves to identify material properties like Young's modulus and bulk modulus.
Apply Hooke's Law qualitatively and quantitatively to analyze deformation in solids.
Calculate elastic potential energy stored in deformed materials.
Apply Pascal's Law to hydraulic systems and analyze pressure variation in fluids under gravity.
Use Stokes' Law and terminal velocity concepts to study viscous drag on spherical bodies.
Explain phenomena like Bernoulli's theorem, surface tension, capillary rise, and excess pressure across curved surfaces.
Differentiate between heat and temperature and calculate calorimetric quantities including specific heats (Cp, Cv).
Analyze phase changes using latent heat concepts and anomalous expansion of water.
Apply conduction, convection, and radiation principles to solve thermal equilibrium problems.
Define temperature using the zeroth law and distinguish between heat, work, and internal energy.
Apply the first law of thermodynamics to various cyclic and non-cyclic processes (isothermal, adiabatic).
Explain the second law of thermodynamics regarding entropy and efficiency of engines.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Behaviour of Perfect Gases and Kinetic Theory of Gases
outcomes: Derive or apply the ideal gas law from kinetic theory assumptions regarding molecular motion.
Interpret temperature as a measure of average kinetic energy (rms speed) of molecules.
Apply the law of equipartition of energy to specific heat capacities and understand mean free path concepts.
Identify conditions for Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M) and derive its equation from restoring force principles.
Analyze the relationship between uniform circular motion and S.H.M including phase concepts.
Calculate time periods of loaded springs and simple pendulums using energy or kinematic methods.
Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves and describe progressive wave characteristics.
Apply the principle of superposition to explain standing waves in strings and organ pipes (harmonics).
Analyze beat formation due to frequency differences and reflection properties of waves.
Apply Coulomb's Law and the superposition principle to calculate forces between multiple charges.
Determine electric field intensity due to point charges, dipoles, continuous distributions (wire, sheet, shell).
Utilize Gauss's Theorem to derive fields for symmetric charge configurations.
Calculate electric potential and potential difference due to point charges, dipoles, and systems of charges.
Analyze the behavior of conductors, insulators, and dielectrics in electrostatic fields including polarization effects.
Compute equivalent capacitance for series/parallel combinations and energy stored in capacitors.
Relate electric current to drift velocity and mobility; analyze V-I characteristics (linear/non-linear).
Apply Ohm's Law and concepts of resistivity/conductivity including temperature effects on resistance.
Solve complex circuits using Kirchhoff's rules, internal resistance analysis, and Wheatstone bridge principles.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism
learning_outcomes.topic: Magnetic Effects of Current - Moving Charges
outcomes: Apply Biot-Savart Law to find magnetic fields due to current elements (e.g., circular loop).
Use Ampere's Circuital Law for symmetric conductors like infinite wires and solenoids.
Analyze forces on moving charges/current-carrying conductors in uniform magnetic fields.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism
outcomes: Model a bar magnet as an equivalent solenoid and calculate fields along its axis/perpendicular bisector.
Classify magnetic materials based on their response to external fields and explain magnetization processes.
Analyze the effect of temperature on magnetic properties of different substances.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents
outcomes: Apply Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction to calculate induced EMF and current direction (Lenz's Law).
Explain the concepts of self-induction and mutual induction in coupled circuits.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents
outcomes: Distinguish between peak/RMS values of AC voltage/current and calculate reactance and impedance in RLC series circuits.
Analyze resonance conditions in AC circuits and the concept of power factor/wattless current.
Explain the working principles of AC generators and transformers.
Understand the concept of displacement current as a source of magnetic fields in changing electric fields.
Describe the transverse nature of EM waves and their propagation characteristics.
Apply mirror formulas and sign conventions to analyze image formation by spherical mirrors.
Use thin lens formulas and lens maker's equation for single lenses and combinations in contact.
Calculate magnifying powers of optical instruments like microscopes and telescopes.
Apply Huygen's Principle to derive laws of reflection/refraction using wave fronts.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit–VII Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
learning_outcomes.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
outcomes: Explain the experimental observations of the photoelectric effect using Einstein's photoelectric equation.
Apply de Broglie relation to calculate wavelength associated with moving particles.
Interpret the results of the alpha-particle scattering experiment leading to the nuclear model of the atom.
Apply Bohr's quantization conditions to derive expressions for radius and energy levels in hydrogen.
Explain the concept of mass defect and calculate binding energy per nucleon for various nuclei.
Distinguish between conductors, insulators, and semiconductors based on energy band theory.
